objective: To adapt peace operations to the evolving nature of conflict and move to more networked multi-dimensional approaches.

howWeGetThere.text: Conduct a comprehensive review of the future of United Nations peace operations and present proposals for a reset for Member State consideration.
Develop practical proposals for delegating delivery of relevant programmatic tasks, with associated resources, to the best placed UN system entities.

progressToDate: The peace operations review will be finalized shortly. It has drawn on extensive consultations with Member States, missions, UN system entities, civil society and independent experts, including written submissions from 47 Member States. This process also included engagement with the General Assembly, the Security Council and relevant subsidiary organs. In the framework of the review, a task force is now developing a concept and roadmap on how to delegate the delivery of relevant programmatic tasks to entities best placed to deliver them, alongside the associated resources.

progressPerAction.text: The peace operations review will be finalized shortly. It has drawn on extensive consultations with Member States, missions, UN system entities, civil society and independent experts, including written submissions from 47 Member States. This process also included engagement with the General Assembly, the Security Council and relevant subsidiary organs.
A task force is developing a concept and roadmap on how to delegate the delivery of relevant programmatic tasks to entities best placed to deliver them, alongside the associated resources.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: The Secretary-General's peace operations review will be presented for the consideration of the General Assembly and the Security Council in June.

The concept and roadmap will be finalized and submitted to the Secretary-General in June. An update will be provided to Member States through a Secretary-General's information brief in July. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

summaryTable.action: Action 15: Conduct a comprehensive review of the future of United Nations peace operations

summaryTable.intergovernmentalConsideration: As relevant, for consideration of General Assembly (eightieth session) and Security Council (2026)

summaryTable.action: Action 14: Delegate relevant programmatic tasks to UN system entities

objective: To make the humanitarian system deliver faster, more efficiently and coherently and with greater impact for people in crisis.

howWeGetThere.text: Simplify humanitarian planning so that Needs and Response Plans are shorter, clearer and more focused on priorities and results.
Integrate supply chains and scale common services so that delivery becomes faster, cheaper and more reliable, feeding into the wider Unified Service Roadmap (Work Package 14) for the UN system.
Strengthen humanitarian data collaboration and interoperability so that agencies can work from a common evidence base, shared beneficiary information, and better-connected data initiatives.
Bring agencies together for coordinated humanitarian diplomacy.
Clarify agency roles and strengthen joint work in food security, mobility and displacement, and health and nutrition.

progressToDate: As part of the IASC-led Humanitarian Reset, humanitarian planning timelines have been shortened by more than 50 days, and core planning documents by 67 per cent. Pilot rollouts for integrated supply chains are under way in Afghanistan, Haiti, the Occupied Palestinian Territory, Somalia and Sudan, including coordinated procurement, global logistics and country-level delivery. A common services catalogue went live in January, with 17 service lines already available. Work is also under way on humanitarian data initiatives, including the Humanitarian Data Collaborative, beneficiary data interoperability, public-private data collaboration and pooled funding for shared data priorities. The humanitarian diplomacy mechanism is already supporting coordinated analysis, messaging and engagement. To further clarify agency roles and strengthen joint work, 20 country teams have reviewed food security arrangements, joint work on mobility and displacement is moving into implementation, and discussions continue on health and nutrition.

progressPerAction.text: Humanitarian planning timelines have been shortened by more than 50 days, and core planning documents by 67 per cent.

Pilot rollouts for integrated supply chains are under way in Afghanistan, Haiti, the Occupied Palestinian Territory, Somalia and Sudan, including coordinated procurement, global logistics and country-level delivery.
A common services catalogue went live in January, with 17 service lines already available.

Work is under way on humanitarian data initiatives, including the Humanitarian Data Collaborative, beneficiary data interoperability, public-private data collaboration and pooled funding for shared data priorities.

The humanitarian diplomacy mechanism is already supporting coordinated analysis, messaging and engagement.
Twenty country teams have reviewed food security arrangements.
Joint work on mobility and displacement is moving into implementation.
Discussions continue on health and nutrition.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Simplified Humanitarian Needs and Response Plans will continue to be rolled out in 2026, with consistent application of the streamlined approach. Updated plans will be available online as they are issued. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.

Integrated supply chain models and common services will continue to be developed in 2026 for subsequent application in a wider range of settings. This will be reflected in the Unified Services Roadmap (Work Package 14) information brief in June. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

Humanitarian data initiatives will move from technical alignment into operational testing. Their concept, roadmap and financing approaches will be brought together in a Secretary-General's information brief in June. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.
The humanitarian diplomacy mechanism will be further operationalized and refined across relevant contexts, as needed. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.

Alignment of agency responsibilities across food security, mobility and displacement, and health and nutrition will continue through 2026, including finalization of a Rome-based agencies collaboration blueprint, pilot-country rollout and further development of joint approaches and interoperability solutions. A Secretary-General's information brief will provide updates on progress in September. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

objective: To assess the potential benefits of a merger between UNDP and UNOPS, to create a stronger engine for sustainable development, with greater scale and impact.

howWeGetThere.text: Conduct a thorough assessment of the potential benefits of a merger between UNDP and UNOPS.

progressToDate: UNDP and UNOPS have developed and submitted a joint analysis of the current state of each entity ("as-is" analysis), which has been shared with Member States. Member States have been briefed, including at the UNDP/UNOPS/UNFPA Executive Board session in February, where decision 2026/2, "Engagement with UN80 initiative", was adopted. Further analysis has been shared separately by the two entities.

progressPerAction.text: UNDP and UNOPS have developed and submitted a joint analysis of the current state of each entity ("as-is" analysis), which has been shared with Member States. Member States have been briefed, including at the UNDP/UNOPS/UNFPA Executive Board session in February, where decision 2026/2, "Engagement with UN80 initiative", was adopted. Further analysis has been shared separately by the two entities.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: UNDP and UNOPS are currently developing a joint evidence-based assessment of the potential benefits of a merger to help determine whether a merger would strengthen the UN development system's ability to deliver results with greater scale and impact. Relevant information will be available for the Executive Board meetings in May and June. Subsequently, the Secretary-General will consider the submission of a report to the General Assembly, including proposals.

summaryTable.action: Action 26: Conduct thorough assessment of potential benefits of a UNDP/UNOPS merger Executive Board; General Assembly
writtenProducts: Secretary-General’s report For General Assembly consideration

objective: To assess the potential benefits of a merger between UNFPA and UN Women and their respective mandates to create a unified voice and platform for gender equality and women's rights.

howWeGetThere.text: Conduct a thorough assessment of the potential benefits of a merger of UNFPA and UN Women and their respective mandates.
Consider options to optimize normative functions of UNFPA on population statistics and related activities.

progressToDate: A full merger assessment report has been shared with Member States at the end of April. It is informed by engagement and consultations with Member States, the Executive Boards of UNFPA and UN Women and civil society partners. It builds on preparatory products published since March, including the current state of each entity ("as-is" analysis), preliminary findings for the assessment, and responses to frequently asked questions. The Executive Boards of UNFPA and UN Women also considered the issue in February, when decisions entitled "Engagement with UN80 initiative" were adopted.

progressPerAction.text: A full merger assessment report has been shared with Member States at the end of April. It builds on preparatory products published since March, including the current state of each entity ("as-is" analysis), preliminary findings for the assessment, and responses to frequently asked questions. The Executive Boards of UNFPA and UN Women also considered the issue in February, when decisions entitled "Engagement with UN80 initiative" were adopted.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: The full merger assessment will be available to the meetings of the Executive Boards in May and June. Subsequently, the Secretary-General will consider the submission of a report to the General Assembly, including proposals.
The analysis considered the division of labour between UNFPA and DESA appropriate and recommended no structural change.

objective: To adapt UN country team configurations to be more impactful and efficient in support of national needs and priorities.

howWeGetThere.text: Develop proposals to reconfigure UN country teams through a system-wide working group, chaired by the Chair of the UN Sustainable Development Group (UNSDG).
Strengthen the Resident Coordinator system to better leverage system-wide knowledge and expertise, including via joint knowledge hubs and the expertise-on-demand mechanism (Work Packages 7, 8).

progressToDate: The working group is developing principles, building blocks and recommendations for country team reconfiguration for the Secretary-General's consideration. In-depth data analysis and consultations with Member States in bilateral, regional group and plenary settings, as well as across the UN system and Resident Coordinators, have informed this process. Co-leads of other relevant work packages have also been consulted. A recalibration of DCO is underway. Member States have provided feedback on how the RC system can be strengthened. Efforts are under way to tailor RC office capacity to best meet UN country team needs.

progressPerAction.text: The working group is developing principles, building blocks and recommendations for country team reconfiguration for the Secretary-General's consideration. In-depth data analysis and consultations with Member States in bilateral, regional group and plenary settings, as well as across the UN system and Resident Coordinators, have informed this process. Co-leads of other relevant work packages have also been consulted.

A recalibration of DCO is under way. Member States have provided feedback on how the RC system can be strengthened. Efforts are under way to tailor RC office capacity to best meet UN country team needs.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Recommendations for the reconfiguration of UN country teams will be finalized by the end of April for the Secretary-General's review and reflected in his report on QCPR implementation to ECOSOC in May and the report of the Chair of the UNSDG in May for Member State consideration.

Relevant proposals to strengthen the Resident Coordinator system will be finalized for the Secretary-General's consideration. They will inform the ongoing recalibration of the Resident Coordinator system that will be presented to the ECOSOC Operational Activities Segment. The General Assembly will also consider the review and financing of the Resident Coordinator system at its eighty-first session, in line with General Assembly resolution 79/258.

objective: To reorganize regional capacities of the UN development system for more effective delivery, better linking global mandates, regional strategies and country-level implementation.

howWeGetThere.text: Develop proposals for a regional reset of capacities for sustainable development, including the establishment of Regional Platforms for Integration (RPI), building upon the existing Regional Collaborative Platforms, to improve internal coordination.
Locate regional capacities more systematically in the premises of Regional Commissions, starting with the regional teams of the Development Coordination Office.

objective: To pool thematic knowledge across the UN development system to strengthen services for Member States.

howWeGetThere.text: Establish Joint Knowledge Hubs that bring together analysis and expertise across UN entities on key themes, providing more joined-up policy support and eliminating duplications.

progressToDate: Over twenty development entities have been consulted and surveys undertaken to design the Joint Knowledge Hubs. Three pilot hubs have been selected for phase 1: Trade and Regional Integration; Productive Transformation; and Strategic Foresight. For each hub, work has started on mapping the expertise and products that the hubs will bring together, agreeing co-leadership arrangements and preparing implementation plans.

progressPerAction.text: Over twenty development entities have been consulted and surveys undertaken to design the Joint Knowledge Hubs. Three pilot hubs have been selected for phase 1: Trade and Regional Integration; Productive Transformation; and Strategic Foresight. For each hub, work has started on mapping the expertise and products that the hubs will bring together, agreeing co-leadership arrangements and preparing implementation plans.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Implementation plans for each pilot hub will be submitted to the Secretary-General by May. The hubs will be reflected in the Secretary-General's QCPR report in May, with further details to be provided in a Secretary-General's information brief by July. The pilot phase will continue until September, with the aim of demonstrating practical results and learning lessons for the next phase. Two additional hubs, on climate change and on science, technology and innovation, are under consideration for phase 2. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.

summaryTable.action: Action 33: Establish joint knowledge hubs

objective: To make UN system expertise rapidly and easily accessible to UN country teams and Member States on demand.

howWeGetThere.text: Establish an expertise-on-demand mechanism with a common catalogue of available expertise and services, clear access protocols and standardized administrative arrangements so that they can be deployed more quickly and efficiently.

progressToDate: The design of the expertise-on-demand mechanism was endorsed by the Secretary-General in February. Since then, work has moved to the build phase, including mapping of expertise and services, design of practical arrangements, circulation of templates and development of the online catalogue.

progressPerAction.text: The design of the expertise-on-demand mechanism was endorsed by the Secretary-General in February. Since then, work has moved to the build phase, including mapping of expertise and services, design of practical arrangements, circulation of templates and development of the online catalogue.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Work will continue on the catalogue, access protocols and standard administrative arrangements. The mechanism will be reflected in the Secretary-General's QCPR report in May, with further details to be provided in a Secretary-General's information brief by September. Initial pilots for the first UN country team will be rolled out by June with support from the Joint SDG Fund. This will be followed by refinement and system-wide operationalization by September. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.

objective: To ensure system-wide coordination so that human rights, in all their dimensions, are fully integrated into UN policies and activities.

howWeGetThere.text: Establish a Human Rights Group as an internal, standing United Nations system mechanism.

progressToDate: Following extensive consultation, the United Nations Human Rights Group has been established by the Secretary-General. It comprises entities from across the United Nations system and is chaired by the High Commissioner for Human Rights.

progressPerAction.text: Following extensive consultation, the United Nations Human Rights Group has been established by the Secretary-General. It comprises entities from across the United Nations system and is chaired by the High Commissioner for Human Rights.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: The Group will hold its first meeting in May. Member States will receive an information brief in June.

objective: To strengthen UN system coordination arrangements and strategic internal oversight of the assignment of implementation responsibilities.

howWeGetThere.text: Assess senior management coordination fora and their administrative support arrangements, as part of the broader assessment on internal strategic oversight (Work Package 20 Action 88).
Review the wider portfolio of interagency coordination mechanisms and task forces at headquarters to reduce duplication and align them with the Regional Monthly Review platform.

progressToDate: Senior management coordination fora and their support arrangements have been mapped. Terms of reference have been prepared to review the operation of these fora, in line with A/RES/80/251. In parallel, a system-wide mapping of more than 200 other coordination mechanisms has been completed to support recommendations on rationalization and realignment.

progressPerAction.text: Senior management coordination fora and their support arrangements have been mapped. Terms of reference have been prepared to review the operation of these fora, in line with A/RES/80/251.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: The review of senior management coordination fora will be completed by September, as part of the broader assessment of how to strengthen internal strategic oversight, including recommendations to the Secretary-General. Member States will receive updates through an information brief on the work package in October.

Recommendations to rationalize the wider portfolio of other interagency coordination mechanisms and task forces will also be submitted to the Secretary-General by September. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.

objective: To ensure the Secretary-General's special envoys, representatives, coordinators and advisers provide optimal impact and efficiency.

howWeGetThere.text: Review special envoy, representative, coordinator and adviser roles to identify redundancies and streamline senior leadership structures.
Abolish the Special Coordinator for Development in the Sahel post and reassign functions to existing entities.

progressToDate: A mapping of other special envoys, representatives, coordinators and advisers has been completed. Secretariat entities have also been asked to review positions at levels D-1 and above and to propose reductions or downward reclassifications in the Proposed Programme Budget for 2027. The position of Special Coordinator for Development in the Sahel was discontinued and the associated office closed at the end of 2025.

objective: To support and empower Resident and Humanitarian Coordinators (RC/HCs) to strengthen cross-pillar collaboration.

howWeGetThere.text: Initiate a time-bound action plan to accelerate co-location of offices, shift to common performance management, clarify management and accountability frameworks and join up strategies for countries in transition.
Simplify the main planning tools used by RC/HCs and their teams, including Cooperation Frameworks, Humanitarian Response Plans and Peacebuilding Strategies.

progressToDate: The action plan has been finalized under joint leadership of the Deputy Secretary-General and the Emergency Relief Coordinator. Co-location of OCHA and Resident Coordinator Offices has been agreed as the standard approach for all 23 locations (43%) where this is not yet the case. The first-ever common performance-management framework for RC/HCs has been finalized. The framework for accountability between RCs and UN country teams is being revised with clearer guidance for crisis settings, alongside improved accountability arrangements for HCs. Joint transition planning has been initiated for eight countries moving out of large-scale humanitarian response, including allocations from the RC System Rapid Release Fund and additional surge capacity.

progressPerAction.text: The action plan has been finalized under joint leadership of the Deputy Secretary-General and the Emergency Relief Coordinator. Co-location of OCHA and Resident Coordinator Offices has been agreed as the standard approach for all 23 locations (43%) where this is not yet the case. The first-ever common performance-management framework for RC/HCs has been finalized. The framework for accountability between RCs and UN country teams is being revised with clearer guidance for crisis settings, alongside improved accountability arrangements for HCs. Joint transition planning has been initiated for eight countries moving out of large-scale humanitarian response, including allocations from the RC System Rapid Release Fund and additional surge capacity.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Implementation of the action plan will be completed by the end of 2026, with first deliverables in place by August. Final parts of the common performance-management system will be ready for launch in 2027. Member States will be informed of progress through a Secretary-General's information brief in May and the Secretary-General's 2026 report on QCPR implementation to ECOSOC in May.

Building on the simplification of Humanitarian Response Plans and improved Cooperation Framework guidance, a dedicated effort will be made to streamline overlapping planning processes. Member States will be informed through a Secretary-General's information brief. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

summaryTable.action: Action 19: Initiate the Shared Platform Initiative
Action 57: Simplify planning tools

objective: To reduce fragmentation in UN system support services and make them more cost-effective.

howWeGetThere.text: Develop a Unified Services Roadmap, supported by a system-wide assessment of where sharing of global services would have the greatest impact.
Integrate supply chains, with the Humanitarian Compact as an early test case for wider service integration.
Scale common back offices at country level so that support can be delivered with less duplication on the ground.
Improve cost transparency through common tools and reports so that efficiency gains can be tracked more clearly.

progressToDate: An outline of the Unified Services Roadmap (USR) has been completed, bringing together the prior work of the High-Level Committee on Management and the Business Innovation Group in a single framework with joint governance and public dashboards. Overall, the USR encompasses far-reaching efficiency initiatives (time-bound projects), global shared services (location-independent services) and common back offices (location-dependent services), across six service categories: procurement and supply chain, finance, ICT, logistics, human resources, and administration. The USR will reinforce horizontal enablers of programme delivery, including staff safety and security. At global level, a study to help sequence the expansion of shared services, and the implementation of the far-reaching efficiency initiatives, have also advanced. At country level, common back offices are being launched in additional countries, with "self-starter" training supporting further expansion. In parallel, the tools and methods needed to strengthen cost transparency have been developed, with country team pilots under way.

progressPerAction.text: An outline of the Unified Services Roadmap (USR) has been completed, bringing together the prior work of the High-Level Committee on Management and the Business Innovation Group in a single framework with joint governance and public dashboards. At global level, a study to help sequence the expansion of shared services, and the implementation of the far-reaching efficiency initiatives, have advanced.

An outline of the Unified Services Roadmap (USR) has been completed, bringing together the prior work of the High-Level Committee on Management and the Business Innovation Group in a single framework with joint governance and public dashboards. At global level, a study to help sequence the expansion of shared services, and the implementation of the far-reaching efficiency initiatives, have advanced.
At country level, common back offices are being launched in additional countries, with self-starter training supporting further expansion.
The tools and methods needed to strengthen cost transparency have been developed, with country team pilots under way.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: The study for global shared services expansion will be finalized by June. The full Unified Services Roadmap and a dashboard will be shared with Member States through a Secretary-General's information brief in June. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

Work on supply-chain integration will continue in 2026 as part of the New Humanitarian Compact (Work Package 2), and expanded beyond initial humanitarian applications on the basis of demonstrated results. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.
Common country back offices will be established in additional countries through 2026. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.
Cost-transparency tools and methods will be rolled out across more country operations by June. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

objective: To have the UN system make better use of shared core technology services, common technology expertise and modern tools.

howWeGetThere.text: Expand the use of shared core ICT services, including for cloud, network and basic enterprise systems, with the UN system making better use of common providers, reducing duplication and improving quality and interoperability.

Establish a Technology Accelerator Platform (TAP) to identify common demands, develop solution portfolios, and mobilize pooled capacity for better shared technology solutions that modernize and upgrade the work of the UN system.

progressToDate: The first UN system-wide ICT baseline survey has been completed and opportunities for greater sharing of core services are being identified. Three initial priority use cases for the TAP have been agreed: AI toolboxes for translation and conferencing, an online platform to enable the work on expertise-on-demand (Work Package 8), and shared digital ID solutions (Work Packages 2 and 14).

progressPerAction.text: The first UN system-wide ICT baseline survey has been completed and opportunities for greater sharing of core services are being identified.

Three initial priority use cases for the TAP have been agreed: AI toolboxes for translation and conferencing, an online platform to enable the work on expertise-on-demand (Work Package 8), and shared digital ID solutions (Work Packages 2 and 14).

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Building on the baseline survey results, an initial outline for sharing of core ICT services will be prepared in May. Following review, a full proposal will be presented to the Secretary-General in June. Implementation will proceed through phased expansion of shared ICT services, as part of the Unified Services Roadmap (Work Package 14), with an information brief for Member States in June.

An initial outline of the TAP concept will be prepared in May. Detailed proposals and implementation plans for the endorsed use cases, including governance, staffing and financing, will be submitted to the Secretary-General by July, for an information brief for Member States in July. Subject to approval and available resources, initial use cases will be active by September. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

objective: To make publicly available UN data easier to access and use, and to build stronger joint data capacity over time.

howWeGetThere.text: Build a shared UN System Data Commons platform so that public data and statistics from different entities can be accessed in one place (data.un.org) and used reliably with AI tools.

Establish a joint programme, with shared governance, resources, teams and tools, to sustain the UN System Data Commons and improve the coherence, quality and future readiness of system-wide data work over time.

progressToDate: An initial Data Commons platform is being developed, formally encouraged by the Statistical Commission, with 25 entities now committed to contribute data to the next phase, as well as know-how or resources. A core delivery team and seed resources have been mobilized. A joint programme proposal is in development to sustain the shared platform and deepen progress on joint standards, production workflows, data maturity and AI readiness.

progressPerAction.text: An initial Data Commons platform is being developed, formally encouraged by the Statistical Commission, with 25 entities committed to contribute data to the next phase, as well as know-how or resources. A core delivery team and seed resources have been mobilized.

A joint programme proposal is in development to sustain the shared platform and deepen progress on joint standards, production workflows, data maturity and AI readiness. A core delivery team and seed resources have been mobilized.

A joint programme proposal is in development to sustain the shared platform and deepen progress on joint standards, production workflows, data maturity and AI readiness. A core delivery team and seed resources have been mobilized.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: The Data Commons platform will be expanded, including the onboarding of datasets from all 25 participating entities, with a public launch in September.

The joint programme proposal will be shared for consideration by participating entities, potential partners and the Secretary-General by July, with phased implementation beginning thereafter, including an information brief in September. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

objective: To make the UN system's training and research activities more coherent, more integrated and more useful.

howWeGetThere.text: Merge UNSSC into UNITAR to create a more coherent and efficient system-wide training and capacity-building offer for UN system personnel and Member States.
Integrate UNRISD into UNU to better align research, strengthen policy relevance and impact.
Establish a joint coordination mechanism to improve coordination of training and research activities across the wider UN system.

progressToDate: For the proposed merger of UNSSC into UNITAR, a structured design process is under way, supported by outside expertise and consultations with Boards, Member States, UN system staff and other stakeholders. For the proposed integration of UNRISD into UNU, consultations on the arrangements are being undertaken, including with the UNRISD Board and the UNU Council. For the coordination mechanism, mapping of training and research functions is complete and a blueprint for the mechanism has been developed.

progressPerAction.text: For the proposed merger of UNSSC into UNITAR, a structured design process is under way, supported by outside expertise and consultations with Boards, Member States, UN system staff and other stakeholders.
For the proposed integration of UNRISD into UNU, consultations on the arrangements are being undertaken, including with the UNRISD Board and the UNU Council.
For the coordination mechanism, mapping of training and research functions is complete and a blueprint for the mechanism has been developed.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: A draft merger proposal will be ready by May, for consideration by the Boards of UNITAR and UNSSC in June. It will be transmitted to the Secretary-General as the basis for his report in July to the General Assembly with a view to a decision during the eightieth session.

An initial integration proposal will be ready for consideration by the UNRISD Board and UNU Council in May and June, respectively. The outcome will be submitted to the Secretary-General for decision. Integration would additionally require a UNU Council decision to establish UNRISD as a UNU institute. Any required revision of the Secretary-General's Bulletin (UNRISD) would follow. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.

The joint system-wide coordination mechanism is being developed by UNU and UNITAR, with a proposed blueprint to be submitted for the Secretary-General's consideration in May. An information brief for Member States will follow in July. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.

objective: To make core and pooled funding more attractive and effective.

howWeGetThere.text: Assess pooled funding arrangements across the UN system and develop recommendations to improve the portfolio of major pooled funds.
Develop proposals to make core funding more attractive.
Develop a more coherent Funding Compact review architecture.

progressToDate: System-wide mapping and analysis have been completed across pooled funding, core funding and Funding Compact review mechanisms. This work has been supported by consultations with Member States, contributors and UN system entities. Initial findings show fragmentation across pooled funds, uneven costing and governance, structural disadvantages affecting core funding, and gaps in current Funding Compact review arrangements.

progressPerAction.text: System-wide mapping and analysis have been completed across pooled funding, core funding and Funding Compact review mechanisms. This work has been supported by consultations with Member States, contributors and UN system entities. Initial findings show fragmentation across pooled funds, uneven costing and governance, structural disadvantages affecting core funding, and gaps in current Funding Compact review arrangements.

No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

objective: To facilitate Member State decision-making by providing enhanced Secretariat services during mandate creation.

howWeGetThere.text: Strengthen Secretariat support to Member States during mandate creation through improved tools, guidance and mapping of existing capacities; enhance information on programme budget implications with system-wide data and tools.
Improve visibility and coordination across mandating organs through system-wide mapping and a shared information platform.

progressToDate: The General Assembly adopted A/RES/80/251 in March, requesting enhanced Secretariat support in connection with mandate creation, implementation and review. Mapping of support currently being provided across mandating organs has been completed. Enhanced functionalities of the mandate registry (Work Package 29), including analytical tools, have been rolled out. Initial work is under way to improve access to information relevant to decision-making across the mandate lifecycle, including on programme budget implications of mandated tasks.

progressPerAction.text: The General Assembly adopted A/RES/80/251 in March, requesting enhanced Secretariat support in connection with mandate creation, implementation and review. Mapping of support currently being provided across mandating organs has been completed. Initial work is under way to improve access to information relevant to decision-making across the mandate lifecycle.

The General Assembly adopted A/RES/80/251 in March, requesting enhanced Secretariat support in connection with mandate creation, implementation and review. Initial work is under way to improve access to information relevant to decision-making across the mandate lifecycle, including on programme budget implications of mandated tasks.

Mapping of support currently being provided across mandating organs has been completed. Enhanced functionalities of the mandate registry (Work Package 29), including analytical tools, have been rolled out.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Action plans to enhance support for mandate creation, including improved capacity and information on budget implications, will be prepared by June in line with A/RES/80/251. The Ad Hoc Working Group on Mandate Implementation Review will continue intergovernmental consideration of relevant issues until April 2027.
A public-facing platform to improve visibility across mandating organs will be initiated in May, with full rollout by August.

objective: To strengthen programme and budget management by clearer assignment of mandate implementation responsibilities across the UN system and stronger internal oversight.

howWeGetThere.text: Assess internal strategic oversight and coordination of the UN system's division of labour so that roles and responsibilities are clearer and duplication is reduced.
Review mandate citations in the UN's Proposed Programme Budget so that entities cite only those mandates for which they have a comparative advantage.
Make better use of programme and budget processes to identify existing deliverables that can be streamlined, consolidated or, where appropriate, proposed for discontinuation.

progressToDate: Revised budget guidance has been issued for the Proposed Programme Budget for 2027, including instructions for the review of mandate citations and deliverables. Early results indicate a substantial improvement in the quantity and relevance of mandate citations. Terms of reference have been prepared for an assessment of ways to strengthen the internal strategic oversight and coordination of the UN system's division of labour.

progressPerAction.text: Terms of reference have been prepared for an assessment of ways to strengthen the internal strategic oversight and coordination of the UN system's division of labour.

Revised budget guidance has been issued for the Proposed Programme Budget for 2027, including instructions for the review of mandate citations. Early results indicate a substantial improvement in the quantity and relevance of mandate citations.
Revised budget guidance has been issued for the Proposed Programme Budget for 2027, including instructions for the review of deliverables.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: The assessment on internal strategic oversight will be submitted to the Secretary-General in September, with an information brief planned in October. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.
The Proposed Programme Budget for 2027 will be finalized by May, reflecting improved mandate citations, and submitted to the General Assembly.
The Proposed Programme Budget for 2027 will show the resulting evolution of deliverables.

summaryTable.action: Action 88: Assess internal strategic oversight and coordination

Action 92: Streamline deliverables

objective: To strengthen and harmonize how organizations in the UN system measure, manage and communicate their results.

howWeGetThere.text: Develop an action plan that will operationalize a system-wide Management for Results (M4R) mechanism that strengthens coherence, transparency and collective accountability across the UN system.

progressToDate: A concept for a practical UN system-wide mechanism for Management for Results has been developed. A draft action plan has been outlined to improve how the link between mandates, resources and results can be articulated, focused on a select number of agreed system-wide results. Consultations with UN system entities and Member States are under way to test the concept, practical options and overall viability of the proposed mechanism.

progressPerAction.text: A concept for a practical UN system-wide mechanism for Management for Results has been developed. A draft action plan has been outlined to improve how the link between mandates, resources and results can be articulated, focused on a select number of agreed system-wide results. Consultations with UN system entities and Member States are under way to test the concept, practical options and overall viability of the proposed mechanism.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: The action plan will be finalized for consideration by the Secretary-General by June, in implementation of resolution A/RES/80/251. A Secretary-General's information brief is planned for July. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

summaryTable.action: Action 93: Develop action plan to strengthen system-wide results management

objective: To build a leaner, more agile and field-oriented peace and security pillar, prioritizing prevention, advancing peacebuilding and political solutions and delivering joined-up impact.

howWeGetThere.text: Streamline and consolidate structures in DPPA and DPO.
Establish a mechanism to link cross-cutting functions.
Integrate UNMHA into OSESGY.
Streamline and align UNOCA and the Office of the Special Envoy for the Great Lakes.
Streamline and align the Office of the Special Adviser on Cyprus with UNFICYP.
Host regional PDAs in Regional Commissions and integrate PDA support in relevant RCOs.
Align surge capacity for peace and security, rule of law, DDR, SSR, small arms control, transitions and prevention of extremist violence.

progressToDate: The implementation of the consolidation and streamlining of key functions and positions that were approved by Member States in A/RES/80/242 is ongoing. With regard to Action 3, DPPA and DPO have strengthened a range of mechanisms at different levels within the Departments dealing with cross-cutting functions. On Action 8, as per S/RES/2813, the Security Council has closed the mandate of UNMHA and requested the Secretary-General to prepare a plan to start the transfer of its residual functions to OSESGY. On Actions 10 and 11, coordination and mapping exercises are advancing to strengthen capacity management of UNFICYP and UNRGID. On Actions 49, 55 and 58, a criticality assessment is regularly conducted by DPPA, UNDP and DCO to determine countries where PDAs are posted, subject to funding availability.

progressPerAction.text: Implementation of the consolidation and streamlining of key functions and positions approved by Member States in A/RES/80/242 is ongoing.

DPPA and DPO have strengthened mechanisms at different levels within the Departments dealing with cross-cutting functions.
As per S/RES/2813, the Security Council has closed the mandate of UNMHA and requested the Secretary-General to prepare a plan to start the transfer of its residual functions to OSESGY.
Coordination and mapping exercises are advancing to strengthen capacity management of UNFICYP and UNRGID.
A criticality assessment is regularly conducted by DPPA, UNDP and DCO to determine countries where PDAs are posted, subject to funding availability.
A criticality assessment is regularly conducted by DPPA, UNDP and DCO to determine countries where PDAs are posted, subject to funding availability.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Implementation of those actions approved by A/RES/80/242 is ongoing throughout 2026. No further decision by an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.
Coordination meetings will continue between DPPA and DPO to strengthen mechanisms to link cross-cutting functions. No decision by an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.
Following the closure of UNMHA in March, OSESGY will assume its residual functions, and its liquidation process will be finalized. No further decision by an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.
A Secretary-General's report will be submitted in May, following which a Security Council decision would be required to align UNOCA and the Office of the Special Envoy for the Great Lakes.
Following the abolishment of the post of Special Adviser on Cyprus (A/RES/80/242), the functions have been carried out by DPPA.
PDAs will be deployed to support conflict prevention activities, subject to funding availability and following criticality assessment. No decision by an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.

A comprehensive mapping of PBPSO surge capacities will be completed and reflected in a Secretary-General's information brief in July. No decision by an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

objective: To improve the accessibility and impact of disarmament analysis and expertise.

howWeGetThere.text: Integrate UNIDIR into ODA, while preserving its research independence, to ensure its analysis informs negotiations and field support more effectively.
Co-locate regional disarmament centres with Regional Commissions.

progressToDate: A joint task force has been established. Initial recommendations to ensure strategic alignment and integrated approaches between UNIDIR and ODA are being prepared. Consultations are ongoing, including informal conversations with Member States and the UNIDIR Board of Trustees. The co-location of disarmament centres with Regional Commissions is under examination in the context of the Regional Reset (Work Package 6).

progressPerAction.text: A joint task force has been established. Initial recommendations to ensure strategic alignment and integrated approaches between UNIDIR and ODA are being prepared. Consultations are ongoing, including informal conversations with Member States and the UNIDIR Board of Trustees.
The co-location of disarmament centres with Regional Commissions is under examination in the context of the Regional Reset (Work Package 6).

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Recommendations will be submitted to the Secretary-General by July, following a UNIDIR Board of Trustees meeting in June. The Secretary-General will consider the submission of a report to the General Assembly, including proposals.

Proposals for co-location arrangements of the regional disarmament centres with Regional Commissions will be developed in the context of the Regional Reset (Work Package 6), with the possibility of inclusion in the Proposed Programme Budget for 2028.

objective: To make UN support to Member States on drugs, crime and counter-terrorism more coherent and effective.

howWeGetThere.text: Integrate UNICRI into UNODC while preserving its research independence, to better connect work on crime prevention, criminal justice and related areas.
Reinforce cooperation on counter-terrorism and violent extremism through joint planning and resource mobilization.
Establish a reporting line from UNODC's Terrorism Prevention Branch to OCT contributing to better coordination on counter-terrorism.

progressToDate: For the proposed integration of UNICRI into UNODC, consultations are under way, including with Member States and the UNICRI Board. For Action 12, proposals have been developed to reinforce cooperation under the Global Counter-Terrorism Coordination Compact. For Action 13, a Secretary-General memorandum is being drafted on reporting lines of the Chief of the Terrorism Prevention Branch in UNODC to the Under-Secretary-General of OCT.

progressPerAction.text: Consultations on the proposed integration of UNICRI into UNODC are under way, including with Member States and the UNICRI Board.
Proposals have been developed to reinforce cooperation under the Global Counter-Terrorism Coordination Compact.
A Secretary-General memorandum is being drafted on reporting lines of the Chief of the Terrorism Prevention Branch in UNODC to the Under-Secretary-General of OCT.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: An initial integration proposal is expected by May, followed by further consultations, including with the UNICRI Board and Member States, through June. The integration will then be considered by CCPCJ in June and by ECOSOC in July. Further to ECOSOC consideration, the Secretary-General would submit, in the Proposed Programme Budget for 2028 regarding UNODC, the detailed arrangements for the integration, for decision by the General Assembly.

Proposals to reinforce cooperation on counter-terrorism will be finalized by the Global Counter-Terrorism Coordination Compact Committee in May, for consideration by the Secretary-General in June. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.
The Secretary-General will take a decision on the reporting lines.

objective: To sustain the UN system's action to end AIDS, sunsetting UNAIDS and mainstreaming its capacity and expertise into relevant entities of the UN development system.

howWeGetThere.text: Develop and implement a transition plan to sunset the Joint UN Programme on HIV/AIDS (UNAIDS) and integrate its capacity and expertise into relevant entities of the UN development system.

progressToDate: The first phase of transformation is under way. This has included UNAIDS Secretariat downsizing, consolidation of the UNAIDS Secretariat country footprint and identification of the 11 cosponsoring organizations as leads or affiliates. The Programme Coordinating Board (PCB) has constituted a working group that has begun its work and has consulted Member States, cosponsoring organizations and UNAIDS Secretariat.

progressPerAction.text: The first phase of transformation is under way. This has included UNAIDS Secretariat downsizing, consolidation of the UNAIDS Secretariat country footprint and identification of the 11 cosponsoring organizations as leads or affiliates. The Programme Coordinating Board (PCB) has constituted a working group that has begun its work and has consulted Member States, cosponsoring organizations and UNAIDS Secretariat.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: The PCB will hold formal multistakeholder consultations, bringing together Member States, civil society and implementing partners in May and September. An interim report of the PCB working group will be discussed by the PCB in June, with a view to preparing a final transformation plan for PCB consideration by October. The outcome would then be transmitted to ECOSOC for decision in November. Separately, specialized agencies that are UNAIDS cosponsoring organizations would be invited to take corresponding decisions regarding their participation.

summaryTable.action: Action 29: Develop and implement a transition plan to sunset UNAIDS and integrate its capacity and expertise into relevant entities of the UN development system

summaryTable.intergovernmentalConsideration: For decisions by ECOSOC and specialized agencies
writtenProducts: Working Group interim report to the PCB (June)
Working Group report on final transformation plan to the PCB (October)

objective: To offer Member States the opportunity and support to explore further potential mergers of UN system entities.

howWeGetThere.text: Provide analysis and technical support on additional merger options at the request of Member States.

progressToDate: To date, no proposals have been put forward beyond those set out in the Secretary-General's report "Shifting Paradigms: United to Deliver" (A/80/392).

progressPerAction.text: To date, no proposals have been put forward beyond those set out in the Secretary-General's report "Shifting Paradigms: United to Deliver" (A/80/392).

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: The Secretary-General stands ready to provide analysis and support on other potential mergers.

objective: To streamline environmental support to Member States for more impactful action.

howWeGetThere.text: Undertake an assessment of current arrangements and make proposals on possible changes and realignments on environmental issues.

progressToDate: Analysis and mapping are under way across four areas: science, governance, coordination and implementation. This will inform the assessment of current arrangements. Initial findings highlight fragmentation and opportunities for stronger coordination, integration and synergies to advance coherence and strengthen global environmental action at all levels.

progressPerAction.text: Analysis and mapping are under way across four areas: science, governance, coordination and implementation. This will inform the assessment of current arrangements. Initial findings highlight fragmentation and opportunities for stronger coordination, integration and synergies to advance coherence and strengthen global environmental action at all levels.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: The analysis and mapping will be completed in April and the assessment by June for presentation to the Secretary-General. The Secretary-General will issue an information brief on the relevant issues arising from the assessment in September. Eventual proposals may be submitted for consideration by intergovernmental organs.

objective: To provide more coherent support to Member States by reducing fragmentation in key development areas.

howWeGetThere.text: Realign macroeconomic analysis capacities across DESA, UNCTAD and the Regional Commissions to provide a single-entry point and joint projections for Member States.
Realign support for LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS across entities to strengthen and optimize organizational arrangements and support for these countries.

progressToDate: A calendar has been established for when and where different parts of the UN Secretariat will publish economic projections. A mapping of macroeconomic capacities is under way. In parallel, Secretariat-wide mapping and consultations have been initiated to assess existing support to LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS.

progressPerAction.text: A calendar has been established for when and where different parts of the UN Secretariat will publish economic projections. A mapping of macroeconomic capacities is under way.
Secretariat-wide mapping and consultations have been initiated to assess existing support to LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: The mapping of macroeconomic analysis capacities will be completed in May to align projections and reporting anchored in joint mechanisms. A joint framework will be developed to designate a single-entry point for macroeconomic analysis. The Secretary-General will review these proposals by September, followed by an information brief.

A proposal for a more coherent support architecture for LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS, including options on governance and division of labour, will be submitted to the Secretary-General by July, followed by an information brief. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

objective: To make mandate information and related tools easier for Member States to find and use.

howWeGetThere.text: Strengthen mandates.un.org as the central UN registry for mandates and improve its links with mandate registries in other entities.
Develop additional digital tools to make mandate-related information across the UN system easier to find and use.

progressToDate: The pilot version of mandates.un.org was launched in October. It gives Member States a clearer view of the nearly 4,000 mandate sources cited by UN entities in support of their Proposed Programme Budget for 2026. Since then, new features have been added, including tools to compare successive versions of resolutions. A timeline has been set for further improvements. More than 100 entities have reviewed the citations for their Proposed Programme Plans for 2027, leading to significant reductions in citations. Work is also under way on additional digital tools, in line with the requests by Member States in A/RES/80/251.

progressPerAction.text: The pilot version of mandates.un.org was launched in October. It gives Member States a clearer view of nearly 4,000 mandate sources cited by UN entities in support of their Proposed Programme Budget for 2026. Since then, new features have been added, including tools to compare successive versions of resolutions. More than 100 entities have reviewed the citations for their Proposed Programme Plans for 2027, leading to significant reductions in citations.
Work is under way on additional digital tools, in line with the requests by Member States in A/RES/80/251.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: mandates.un.org will be improved by June, including the updated mandate citations, new functionality, and stronger links with mandate registries in other UN system entities, in line with A/RES/80/251. No further decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

Additional digital tools will be introduced in stages, including an improved online chart of the UN system by June, as well as common data standards for registries by December, as requested in A/RES/80/251.

objective: To improve the clarity, relevance and usability of reporting by the Secretary-General.

howWeGetThere.text: Issue and update internal guidance on word limits so that reports are shorter, clearer and easier to read; develop updated internal guidelines on reporting formats for different types of requests by Member States.
Review overlap and fragmentation in reporting and submit proposals to reduce duplication.
Display document download counts on the UN Official Document System (ODS) and the Digital Library sites.

progressToDate: New internal guidelines have been prepared for word limits and report formats, guided by A/RES/80/251. Opportunities for streamlining the Secretary-General's reporting have also been identified. Steps were completed to launch the display of download counts.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Internal guidance on word limits and reporting formats will be finalized and disseminated to staff in May. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.

Proposals for adjustments to reporting, including possible combination of reports or changes in periodicity, will be presented to the Ad Hoc Working Group on Mandate Implementation Review by the end of June, in accordance with paragraphs 16 and 33 (d) (ii) of A/RES/80/251.
Document download counts will be displayed on UN ODS and the UN Digital Library websites starting in May. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.

objective: To make UN Secretariat administrative services more efficient and effective by reducing duplication and consolidating services.

howWeGetThere.text: Implement the efficiency measures already approved in December 2025 in resolution A/RES/80/242.
Consolidate other administrative services such as travel processing, accounts payable, human resources, and ICT so that service delivery is more streamlined.

progressToDate: In 2026, as approved in A/RES/80/242, Common Administrative Platforms (CAPs) were launched in New York and Bangkok to consolidate previously fragmented work. A global payroll processing team was also established in New York, Nairobi and Entebbe to unify this service. Analytical work has been initiated for other administrative service improvements.

progressPerAction.text: In 2026, as approved in A/RES/80/242, Common Administrative Platforms (CAPs) were launched in New York and Bangkok to consolidate previously fragmented work. A global payroll processing team was also established in New York, Nairobi and Entebbe to unify this service.
Analytical work has been initiated for other administrative service improvements.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Expansion of CAPs to additional duty stations will continue in 2026-2028, alongside full consolidation of payroll processing in the second half of 2026. Initial progress will be reflected in budget proposals presented to the General Assembly for 2027 and 2028.

Analytical work will continue for the consolidation of travel processing, ICT, human resources administration and other administrative processes in 2026. Proposals will be reflected in future budget proposals for consideration of the General Assembly.
